blocks.text: Hamath also, which borders on it, Tyre and Sidon, though they are very wise.
2 : , Phoenician port cities, the subject of earlier oracles against the nations (e.g., Isa 23; Ezek 26–28) and also important mercantile centers in the Persian period (Ezra 3.7; Neh 13.16).
Tyre has built itself a rampart, and heaped up silver like dust, and gold like the dirt of the streets.
But now, the Lord will strip it of its possessions and hurl its wealth into the sea, and it shall be devoured by fire.
Ashkelon shall see it and be afraid; Gaza too, and shall writhe in anguish; Ekron also, because its hopes are withered. The king shall perish from Gaza; Ashkelon shall be uninhabited;
a mongrel people shall settle in Ashdod, and I will make an end of the pride of Philistia.
5–6 : The sites are sometimes personified— Verses 5–7 highlight Philistine cities.
I will take away its blood from its mouth, and its abominations from between its teeth; it too shall be a remnant for our God; it shall be like a clan in Judah, and Ekron shall be like the Jebusites.
Then I will encamp at my house as a guard, so that no one shall march to and fro; no oppressor shall again overrun them, for now I have seen with my own eyes.

7–8 : God now speaks in the first person, promising to remove (cf. Lev 7.21; 11.10–12) from Philistia's mouth, so that the Philistines will observe Jewish dietary laws (cf. Gen 9.4; Lev 11.2–47; Deut 14.3–21). , pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem; see Josh 15.63; 2 Sam 5.6–9; 1 Chr 11.4–9.
8 : , an unusual word to depict God's presence at the Temple. It continues the military tone of the poem, with the as lit., “as a garrison”).
Rejoice greatly, O daughter Zion! Shout aloud, O daughter Jerusalem! Lo, your king comes to you; triumphant and victorious is he, humble and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.

9 : , see Lam 1.6; Zeph 3.14–15; Zech 2.13. (i.e., on one animal described in two parallel poetic lines); cf. Mt 21.2–7, which understands there are to be two animals. , probably the Euphrates. The demilitarized dominion described here is similar to Mic 4.3; Isa 2.4.

He will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the war-horse from Jerusalem; and the battle bow shall be cut off, and he shall command peace to the nations; his dominion shall be from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth.
9–10 : The king welcomed.
As for you also, because of the blood of my covenant with you, I will set your prisoners free from the waterless pit.
11 : is feminine singular, referring still to echoes Ex 24.8, the Sinai covenant.
Return to your stronghold, O prisoners of hope; today I declare that I will restore to you double.
For I have bent Judah as my bow; I have made Ephraim its arrow. I will arouse your sons, O Zion, against your sons, O Greece, and wield you like a warrior's sword.
11–13 : Oracle advocating return and preparation for battle.
13 : Martial imagery returns, with and , the preexilic Southern and Northern Kingdoms, as weapons. , along with Joel 3.6, the earliest reference in the Bible to this enemy of Persia.
Then the Lord will appear over them, and his arrow go forth like lightning; the Lord God will sound the trumpet and march forth in the whirlwinds of the south.
14 : A military theophany. , cf. Judg 5.4.
The Lord of hosts will protect them, and they shall devour and tread down the slingers; they shall drink their blood like wine, and be full like a bowl, drenched like the corners of the altar.
14–15 : The battle and its aftermath are described.
15 : Typical of holy war, the divine warrior will fight and Israel will reap the benefits of victory, here described in graphic imagery, which also alludes to the Temple— (Ex 27.3), (Ex 27.2).
On that day the Lord their God will save them for they are the flock of his people; for like the jewels of a crown they shall shine on his land.
For what goodness and beauty are his! Grain shall make the young men flourish, and new wine the young women.
9.1–11.17: The first “Oracle.” “ ” also appears in Zech 12.1; and Mal 1.1., suggesting that three “oracles” have been appended to Zechariah 1–8.
9.9–17: God's sovereign rule. A series of poetic vignettes about a future king and God's victory.
16–17 : The as royal shepherd, using the metaphor of a flock and a simile of jewelry. symbolize fertility; cf. Hag 2.19.
